unit.name: 3D geometry begins with explicit representations
unit.description: Introduces why 3D objects are harder to encode than 2D images and motivates point clouds and polygon meshes as foundational explicit representations.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by framing 3D vision as a representation problem: unlike images, 3D objects vary widely in scale, geometry, texture, and material, so there is no single analogue of pixels [ts=95s][ts=129s]. Wu organizes geometry representations into explicit and implicit families, then dives into point clouds as unordered sets of 3D coordinates, optionally with normals; oriented points are called surfels, and normals matter for realistic lighting and shading [ts=156s][ts=301s][ts=340s][ts=365s]. He emphasizes that point clouds often come directly from scanners and depth sensors, but can be noisy, unevenly sampled, and missing topology, which makes undersampled regions hard to render and leaves ambiguity about surface connectivity [ts=434s][ts=480s]. Polygon meshes address this by storing both points and their connectivity, making them the dominant representation in graphics and games and enabling operations like subdivision, simplification, and regularization, though their irregular structure historically made them harder to integrate with early CNN-based deep learning [ts=508s][ts=613s][ts=661s].

key_points.text: 3D object geometry is commonly divided into explicit and implicit representations.
A point cloud is an unordered set of 3D coordinates; adding orientation yields surfels.
Surface normals are needed to render point-based geometry realistically under lighting.
Point clouds often come from scanners and depth sensors and can be noisy or require registration.
Point clouds lack topological information, so connectivity ambiguities remain.
Polygon meshes store both vertices and face connectivity, enabling subdivision, simplification, and regularization.

kp_catalog.name: Explicit vs. implicit geometry taxonomy
kp_catalog.description: The organizing distinction between representations that directly enumerate or parametrize surface points and representations that define geometry through a function or field.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because explicit vs. implicit geometry taxonomy formalizes how the organizing distinction between representations that directly enumerate or parametrize surface points and representations that define geometry through a function or field, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Point clouds
kp_catalog.description: An unordered set of 3D points, optionally with normals, often produced directly by sensors and useful as a flexible but topology-free representation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because point clouds clarifies an unordered set of 3D points, optionally with normals, often produced directly by sensors and useful as a flexible but topology-free representation, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Polygon meshes
kp_catalog.description: A boundary representation that adds connectivity and faces to sampled points, enabling rich geometric operations and practical graphics pipelines.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because polygon meshes clarifies a boundary representation that adds connectivity and faces to sampled points, enabling rich geometric operations and practical graphics pipelines, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
